content:
# Acts Chapter 23 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 254-255 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_023_page_001.jpg, ACT_023_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And having looked intently at the Sanhedrin, Paul said, "Men, brothers, I in all good conscience have conducted-myself-as-a-citizen before God until this day."

**2** And the high priest Ananias commanded those standing-beside him to strike his mouth.

**3** Then Paul said to him, "God is about to strike you, [you] whitewashed wall! And you sit judging me according to the law, and transgressing-the-law you command me to be struck?"

**4** And those standing-by said, "The high priest of God do you revile?"

**5** And Paul said, "I had not known, brothers, that he is high priest; for it has been written, 'A ruler of your people you shall not speak-evil-of.'"

**6** And Paul having known that the one part is of Sadducees and the other of Pharisees, was crying out in the Sanhedrin, "Men, brothers, I am a Pharisee, a son of Pharisees; concerning [the] hope and resurrection of [the] dead I am being judged."

**7** And this having been said by him, there came-about a dissension of the Pharisees and Sadducees, and the multitude was divided.

**8** For Sadducees say there is no resurrection, nor angel, nor spirit; but Pharisees confess both [of them].

**9** And there came-about a great clamor, and having risen-up, some of the scribes of the part of the Pharisees were striving-through [it], saying, "Nothing evil we find in this man; and if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel—"

**10** And much dissension having come-about, the commander, having feared lest Paul should be torn-apart by them, commanded the soldiers having gone-down to seize him out of [the] midst of them and to bring [him] into the barracks.

**11** And the following night the Lord having stood-by him said, "Take courage; for as you solemnly-testified the [things] concerning me in Jerusalem, so it is necessary [for] you also in Rome to testify."

**12** And day having come-about, the Jews having made a conspiracy bound themselves under a curse, saying neither to eat nor to drink until which [time] they should kill Paul.

**13** And they were more than forty, the ones having made this sworn-plot.

**14** Who having come to the chief priests and the elders said, "With a curse we have cursed ourselves, to taste nothing until which [time] we should kill Paul.

**15** Now therefore you, make known to the commander together with the Sanhedrin, so that he might bring him down to you, as being about to determine more accurately the [things] concerning him; and we, before the [time] for him to draw near, are ready to kill him."

**16** And having heard [of] the ambush, the son of the sister of Paul, having come and having entered into the barracks, reported [it] to Paul.

**17** And Paul having called-to [him] one of the centurions said, "This young-man lead to the commander, for he has something to report to him."

**18** Indeed therefore having taken him, he brought [him] to the commander and says, "The prisoner Paul having called me asked [me] to bring this young-man to you, having something to speak to you."

**19** And having taken-hold of his hand, the commander, having withdrawn privately, was inquiring, "What is it that you have to report to me?"

**20** And he said, "The Jews agreed to ask you that tomorrow you should bring-down Paul into the Sanhedrin, as being about to inquire something more accurately concerning him.

**21** You therefore, do not be persuaded by them; for there lie-in-wait for him of them more than forty men, who bound themselves under a curse neither to eat nor to drink until which [time] they should kill him; and now they are ready, awaiting the promise from you."

**22** Indeed therefore the commander dismissed the young-man, having charged [him], "To no one tell that you disclosed these [things] to me."

**23** And having called-to [him] certain two of the centurions, he said, "Prepare soldiers two hundred, so that they might go as far as Caesarea, and horsemen seventy, and spearmen two hundred, from [the] third hour of the night;

**24** and beasts-of-burden to set-upon, so that having mounted Paul they might bring-safely-through to Felix the governor."

**25** Having written a letter having this form:

**26** "Claudius Lysias to the most excellent governor Felix, greetings.

**27** This man having been seized by the Jews and being about to be killed by them, having come-upon [them] with the soldiers I rescued, having learned that he is a Roman.

**28** And wanting to know the charge on account of which they were accusing him, I brought [him] down into their Sanhedrin;

**29** whom I found being accused concerning questions of their law, and having no charge worthy of death or of bonds.

**30** And [it] having been disclosed to me [that] a plot would be against the man, at once I sent to you, having commanded also the accusers to speak the [things] against him before you."

**31** Indeed therefore the soldiers, according to the [thing] having been commanded them, having taken-up Paul, brought [him] through the night to Antipatris;

**32** and on the morrow, having allowed the horsemen to go on with him, they returned to the barracks.

**33** Who, having entered into Caesarea and having delivered the letter to the governor, presented also Paul to him.

**34** And having read [it] and having asked from what province he is, and having learned that [he is] from Cilicia,

**35** "I will hear you fully," he said, "when also your accusers arrive." And he commanded him to be guarded in the praetorium of Herod.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **Verse 1 -- "conducted-myself-as-a-citizen" (pepoliteumai):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΠΟΛΙΤΕΥΜΑΙ, the perfect middle/passive of politeuomai, meaning specifically "to live as a citizen." This is stronger than a generic "lived" -- Paul claims civic-quality conduct before God. The perfect tense indicates a completed state with present results.

2. **Verse 3 -- "whitewashed wall":** Sinaiticus preserves ΤΟΙΧΕ ΚΕΚΟΝΙΑΜΕΝΕ. The perfect passive participle kekoniamene ("having been whitewashed") echoes Jesus' denunciation of whitewashed tombs (Matt 23:27). Paul's insult implies outward legal purity concealing inner corruption.

3. **Verse 5 -- "I had not known" (ouk edein):** Sinaiticus reads OUK ΗΔΕΙΝ, the pluperfect of oida. Whether Paul genuinely did not recognize Ananias as high priest, or speaks with irony, remains debated. The pluperfect suggests a past state of not-knowing.

4. **Verse 6 -- Sinaiticus variant:** Sinaiticus reads ΥΙΟΣ ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΩΝ ("son of Pharisees," plural) rather than the singular ΦΑΡΙΣΑΙΟΥ found in some later manuscripts. This plural form emphasizes Paul's multi-generational Pharisaic lineage.

5. **Verse 8 -- "both" (amphotera):** Sinaiticus reads ΑΜΦΟΤΕΡΑ, which strictly means "both [of two]," though the Sadducees deny three things (resurrection, angel, spirit). Some scholars take "angel" and "spirit" as a single category of spiritual beings, making it genuinely two items denied.

6. **Verse 9 -- "if a spirit spoke to him, or an angel":** The sentence breaks off in an aposiopesis (deliberate trailing off). Sinaiticus preserves this unfinished conditional without adding a completion, reflecting the tumult of the scene.

7. **Verse 11 -- "the Lord" (ho Kyrios):** Sinaiticus reads Ο ΚΥΡΙΟΣ. This is a christophany -- the risen Christ appearing to Paul at night. The verb ΕΠΙΣΤΑΣ ("having stood by") indicates a sudden, personal appearance.

8. **Verse 12 -- "bound themselves under a curse" (anethematisan heautous):** Sinaiticus uses ΑΝΕΘΕΜΑΤΙΣΑΝ, meaning they placed themselves under a self-imprecatory oath (anathema). This was a solemn invocation of divine punishment upon themselves should they fail.

9. **Verse 23 -- "spearmen" (dexiolabous):** The term ΔΕΞΙΟΛΑΒΟΥΣ is a hapax legomenon in the New Testament. Its exact meaning is debated: possibly "those who seize with the right hand," interpreted as light-armed spearmen, slingers, or bodyguards. Sinaiticus clearly preserves this rare term.

10. **Verse 25 -- "having this form" (periechosan ton tupon touton):** Sinaiticus reads ΠΕΡΙΕΧΟΥΣΑΝ ΤΟΝ ΤΥΠΟΝ ΤΟΥΤΟΝ. Luke provides a paraphrase rather than a verbatim copy of the official Latin document. The word tupon ("form, pattern") indicates an approximate rendering of the letter's content.

11. **Verse 27 -- Lysias's distortion:** The commander's letter to Felix reorders events to his advantage. He implies he rescued Paul because he learned he was Roman, when in fact he learned of Paul's citizenship only after ordering him bound and flogged (22:25-29). Sinaiticus preserves this self-serving narrative without editorial correction.

12. **Verse 30 -- Sinaiticus reading:** Sinaiticus includes the sending of the accusers as part of the letter. Some manuscripts add "Farewell" (ΕΡΡΩΣΟ) at the end of verse 30; the presence or absence of this epistolary closing varies in the manuscript tradition.

13. **Verse 31 -- Antipatris:** The city lies approximately 35 miles (56 km) northwest of Jerusalem on the road to Caesarea. The overnight forced march with 470 soldiers underscores the seriousness of the threat against Paul.

14. **Verse 35 -- "praetorium of Herod":** Sinaiticus reads ΕΝ ΤΩ ΠΡΑΙΤΩΡΙΩ ΤΟΥ ΗΡΩΔΟΥ. This was the palace built by Herod the Great in Caesarea, now serving as the Roman governor's headquarters. Paul is held in official Roman custody, not a common prison.

-- EMET
